content.problemStatement: Swap SortYou are given an array AAA of size nnn (not exceeding 500500500), you are allowed a maximum of n−1n-1n−1 swaps where in each swap you can choose **any two** indices i,ji, ji,j such that 0≤i,j≤n−10 \leq i, j \leq n-10≤i,j≤n−1 and swap the values of AiA_iAi​ and AjA_jAj​.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 500). The second line contains n space‑separated integers A0 … A{n‑1} (−10^9 ≤ Ai ≤ 10^9).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output the number of performed swaps m on a single line. Then output m lines, each containing two integers i and j (0 ≤ i, j < n) denoting a swap of positions i and j. After applying the swaps in the given order the array must be sorted in non‑decreasing order.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Only one swap (positions 0 and 2) is needed to sort the array.
Selection‑sort swaps: (0,1) puts 1 at front, (1,3) puts 2 next, (2,4) puts 3, (3,4) puts 4. The array becomes [1,2,3,4,5].
A single element array is already sorted; no swaps are required.

solution.approach: We use a simple selection‑sort style algorithm. For each position i from 0 to n‑2 we find the smallest element among indices i … n‑1. If the smallest element is not already at i we swap A[i] with that element and record the swap (i, pos). This uses at most n‑1 swaps, satisfying the problem limit. The algorithm is deterministic and works for any integer values, including negatives and duplicates.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * n^2) – for each test case we scan the unsorted suffix to find the minimum.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) besides the input array and the list of recorded swaps.
